items.title: RT Capitalmind: Most portfolios are built as though somebody knows which market cap wins next. Nobody does. We looked at 15 years of Nifty data on lar...
items.description_html:
RT Capitalmind
Most portfolios are built as though somebody knows which market cap wins next. Nobody does.

We looked at 15 years of Nifty data on large, mid, and small caps.

CAGR since Jan 2011:
● Nifty Mid Cap 150: 15.8% CAGR
● Nifty Small Cap 250: 12.9% CAGR
● Nifty 50: 10.6% CAGR

But CAGR hides the rotation.
Small caps won 7 of the last 15 calendar years.
Large caps won 6.
Mid caps won 2.

Large caps tend to win the down years (2011, 2018, 2022), small caps take the rally years, and mid caps quietly compound through both.

The case for owning all three isn't that each maximizes something. It's that they disagree with each other often enough to keep you in your seat.

Full piece with the drawdowns, rolling returns and correlation data: https://www.capitalmind.in/insights/why-does-your-equity-portfolio-need-diversification-across-market-caps

items.title: India’s Presidency of BRICS has come at a very pivotal time for the global economy. It was in this context that the BRICS Academic Mid‑Term Conferen...
items.description_html:
India’s Presidency of BRICS has come at a very pivotal time for the global economy. It was in this context that the BRICS Academic Mid‑Term Conference in Dehradun, on 29 May 2026, assumed a lot of significance and enthusiastic participation from across the BRICS member countries plus partner nations.

CareEdge Global and the Observer Research Foundation launched an important new report at the Conference: “Breaking convention: How CareEdge Global Ratings is redefining global credit risk assessment.” The report — unveiled by MD and Group CEO of CareEdge - Mr. Mehul Pandya; Vice‑Chancellor, Doon University, Dehradun - Dr. Prof Surekha Dangwal, and Vice President - ORF and Executive Director, BRICS Think Tank Council - India, Dr. Harsh V. Pant — lays out a forward‑looking framework that challenges traditional credit paradigms and addresses geopolitical, climate, and structural risks shaping sovereign and corporate credit today. The event also featured an excellent keynote address by Shri Shambhu L. Hakki, Jt. Secretary (MER) and BRICS Sous-Sherpa for India.

A proud moment for collaboration between academia, policy, and industry. Read the report to understand the next frontier in credit risk thinking.

#CareEdgeGlobal #ORF #BRICS #SovereignRisk #CreditRatings

@BRICSinfo @MEAIndia @SudhakarDalela @shambhuhakki @CareEdge_Group @orfonline @SamirSaran @ShubhSoni @JhanviTripathi @RevatiKasture @jainaakaash

items.description_html:
During the second panel discussion on Global Debt at a Crossroads: Re-pricing Risk in a Fragmented World at the Debt Market Summit 2026, organised by CareEdge Ratings, the panellists were focused on the evolving dynamics of India’s debt markets, particularly sovereign and corporate bond issuances, ECBs, and the broader role of domestic and global investors.

A key takeaway was the strong investor response to recent sovereign and corporate bond issuances, driven by confidence in India’s macro stability and improving corporate balance sheets. Participants noted that issuers are increasingly testing the market with longer tenors and tighter pricing, reflecting growing maturity and depth in the debt capital markets.

Watch the full discussion here: https://lnkd.in/dDxG6uCX

items.title: RT ANI: #WATCH | Mumbai, Maharashtra: National Stock Exchange of India (NSE) Managing Director and CEO Ashishkumar Chauhan attends the CareEdge Debt M...
items.description_html:
RT ANI
#WATCH | Mumbai, Maharashtra: National Stock Exchange of India (NSE) Managing Director and CEO Ashishkumar Chauhan attends the CareEdge Debt Market Summit 2026.

He says, “There is an old saying among bridge builders: the structure is only as strong as its weakest pillar. India's financial architecture has built an impressive equity market infrastructure over the last three and a half decades. The corporate bond market is the pillar that must now be strengthened to the same standard…The question before us is how far we have progressed and how much further we need to go. The progress is real and measurable. India's outstanding corporate bond market has expanded at an annualised pace of 13.6% to ₹59.1 lakh crore in Financial Year 2026, growing faster than the overall bank credit growth of 12.1% over the same period. As a share of GDP, corporate bonds now stand at 17.1%, up from 11.7% in Financial Year 2012… Corporate bonds represent 21.5% of the overall domestic debt market. It's meaningful, but not yet transformative…”

items.title: Retail investors are crushing Wall Street: Stocks most favored by retail investors outperformed those most favored by mutual funds by 16 percentage po...
items.description_html:
Retail investors are crushing Wall Street:

Stocks most favored by retail investors outperformed those most favored by mutual funds by 16 percentage points in May, the largest gap since data began in 2018.

The retail favorite stock basket covers mega-cap tech names, including the Magnificent 7 and semiconductor stocks.

This follows a 14-point outperformance seen in April, making it the 2-month largest gap on record.

This also exceeds the previous 13 percentage point record seen in late 2020, during the early stages of the meme stock mania.

Meanwhile, the total amount of money spent on semiconductor options contracts by retail investors is up to 4.9 TIMES the historical monthly average, the highest on record.

This is also ~25% above the prior record set in March 2024.

Retail investors are loving this market.

items.title: BREAKING: President Trump calls for a physical audit of Fort Knox's gold reserves. The US reports holding 147.3 million troy ounces of gold at Fort Kn...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: President Trump calls for a physical audit of Fort Knox's gold reserves.

The US reports holding 147.3 million troy ounces of gold at Fort Knox, worth $500+ billion.

Yet, Fort Knox has not undergone an independent physical audit in over 70 years.

items.title: BREAKING: President Trump says he is "in no hurry" to make a deal with Iran and "if we don't get what we want, we are going to end it in a different w...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: President Trump says he is "in no hurry" to make a deal with Iran and "if we don't get what we want, we are going to end it in a different way."

Last weekend, President Trump said a deal was going to be announced "shortly."

Today marks 93 days since the Iran War began.

items.title: Americans have rarely been this bullish on equities: In December, 55% of US consumers expected higher stock prices over the next 12 months, the 3rd hi...
items.description_html:
Americans have rarely been this bullish on equities:

In December, 55% of US consumers expected higher stock prices over the next 12 months, the 3rd highest monthly reading since this question was first asked in 1987.

Since April 2025, bullish sentiment has surged +17 percentage points and is just shy of its all-time high set in December 2024.

By comparison, this metric peaked at ~47% during the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble and at ~51% in 2018.

This is also well above the 49-year average of 36%.

Meanwhile, only 25% of consumers expect lower stock prices over the next year, near the lowest since 2020.

This metric stands below its long-term average of 28% and the March 2025 high of 47%.

Bullish sentiment is incredibly strong.

items.title: Inflation is increasingly outpacing American incomes: Real disposable income fell -1.1% YoY in April, the biggest decline since October 2022. On an an...
items.description_html:
Inflation is increasingly outpacing American incomes:

Real disposable income fell -1.1% YoY in April, the biggest decline since October 2022.

On an annualized MoM basis, this metric dropped -5.6%, the biggest decline since May 2025.

Furthermore, real personal income excluding government transfer payments fell -0.4%, the 4th monthly decline in 5 months.

This metric captures after-tax income from labor and capital sources while excluding government support such as Social Security and unemployment benefits.

Over the last 6 months, real personal income excluding transfer payments has declined by an annualized -1.8%, the largest drop since 2022.

Historically, such weakness has rarely occurred outside of a recession or an economic slowdown.

Inflation is eroding the purchasing power of most Americans.

items.title: US tech stocks are rewriting the record books: The Information Technology Sector now accounts for 37% of the S&P 500’s market cap, an all-time high. ...
items.description_html:
US tech stocks are rewriting the record books:

The Information Technology Sector now accounts for 37% of the S&P 500’s market cap, an all-time high.

This percentage has nearly DOUBLED since the 2020 market bottom.

To put this into perspective, the 2000 Dot-Com Bubble peak was 35%.

As a result, the tech sector's market cap is now up to a record $29 trillion.

Technology stocks also account for a record 91% of total US GDP.

Meanwhile, the remaining 10 sectors collectively account for $49 trillion of the $78 trillion in US market capitalization.

The market has never been more driven by one sector.

items.title: World central banks are increasingly moving toward tightening: Global central banks have cut rates 31 times year-to-date, outpacing 12 hikes conducted...
items.description_html:
World central banks are increasingly moving toward tightening:

Global central banks have cut rates 31 times year-to-date, outpacing 12 hikes conducted over the same period.

Over the last 3 months, there were just ~3 more rate cuts than hikes globally, the smallest gap since 2023.

Developed markets have now implemented 3 more rate hikes than cuts over this period.

At the same time, emerging markets have still seen 6 more rate cuts than hikes.

To put this into perspective, world central banks hiked rates ~90 times more than they cut in a single 3-month period during the peak of the 2022 inflation crisis.

Resurgent inflation points to more rate hikes ahead.

items.title: China's shrinking appetite for oil is balancing global crude markets: China's crude oil imports are tracking at ~6.6 million barrels per day so far in...
items.description_html:
China's shrinking appetite for oil is balancing global crude markets:

China's crude oil imports are tracking at ~6.6 million barrels per day so far in May, the lowest since 2016/

This comes as oil inbound shipments fell -20% MoM in April, to 9.37 million barrels per day, the lowest since July 2022.

This compares to 11.6 million barrels per day in 2025, when the country was aggressively stockpiling to bolster energy security.

China’s crude oil imports are now expected to average 10.9 million barrels per day in 2026, the lowest average for any year since 2022.

However, China's oil inventories have declined by only ~20 million barrels over the last several weeks, to ~1.23 billion barrels, still +15% above early 2025 levels.

Over the prior 2 years, the country’s stockpiling absorbed much of the world's surplus oil and helped support global prices, but this dynamic has now reversed and is capping any further upside.

China is effectively putting a ceiling on oil price gains despite severe supply shortages.

items.title: BREAKING: The US personal savings rate fell -0.6 percentage points in April, to 2.6%, the lowest since June 2022. This marks the 3rd consecutive month...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: The US personal savings rate fell -0.6 percentage points in April, to 2.6%, the lowest since June 2022.

This marks the 3rd consecutive monthly decline for a cumulative drop of -1.7 percentage points.

This is also the 2nd lowest reading since April 2008.

This comes as consumer spending surged +5.7% YoY in April, outpacing personal income growth of +2.5% YoY, the widest gap since 2022.

This also marks the 12th consecutive month that consumer spending growth has outpaced income growth.

Put simply, rising costs of living are increasingly forcing consumers to cut into their savings to keep up.

American households are running out of savings as inflation takes its toll on their budgets.

items.title: The US debt crisis is set to become significantly worse: Entitlements, interest, and other mandatory outlays are estimated to consistently surpass Fed...
items.description_html:
The US debt crisis is set to become significantly worse:

Entitlements, interest, and other mandatory outlays are estimated to consistently surpass Federal tax revenues by 2031, according to CBO.

These expenditures are projected to rise to ~20% of GDP by 2040.

That would be the highest percentage on record when excluding the 2020 and 2021 pandemic period.

Net interest alone is estimated to reach 5% of GDP, an all-time high.

At the same time, tax revenues are expected to increase only modestly, to 18% of GDP, the highest since 2020.

To put this into perspective, tax revenues as a % of GDP exceeded entitlements, interest, and other mandatory outlays by 8 percentage points in 2000.

What is the long-term plan here?

items.title: Such a divergence has never been seen in markets: The S&P 500's 3-month implied correlation is down to ~15.0%, the lowest on record. Implied correlati...
items.description_html:
Such a divergence has never been seen in markets:

The S&P 500's 3-month implied correlation is down to ~15.0%, the lowest on record.

Implied correlation measures the extent to which stocks are expected to move in tandem, with a reading this low indicating significantly greater and independence among individual stocks within the index.

At the same time, the 12-month implied correlation of the Nasdaq 100 is down to 25.5%, also an all-time low.

This percentage has declined -35 points since 2022 and is well below the long-term average of 45.9%.

This is consistent with a highly concentrated market environment dominated by a narrow group of large-cap technology stocks.

Market leadership has rarely been this narrow.

items.title: AI is about to reshape the global workforce: 24% of jobs globally, or ~838 million positions, are estimated to be exposed to generative AI. High-incom...
items.description_html:
AI is about to reshape the global workforce:

24% of jobs globally, or ~838 million positions, are estimated to be exposed to generative AI.

High-income countries are facing the greatest risk at ~33% of employment, with ~6% of roles potentially having the highest exposure.

Jobs with the highest exposure are those with repetitive and predictable tasks, meaning AI can automate them with little human variability required.

By comparison, the exposure of low-income countries is ~11% or one-third the levels of high-income countries.

Europe and Central Asia are the most exposed regions at ~32% of employment, followed by the Americas at ~30%, while Africa is the least exposed at ~13%.

The biggest workforce transformation in modern history is underway.

items.title: AI is rapidly expanding in education. A recent study showed 85% of teachers and 86% of students used AI during the 2024-25 school year. And, teachers ...
items.description_html:
AI is rapidly expanding in education.

A recent study showed 85% of teachers and 86% of students used AI during the 2024-25 school year.

And, teachers who use AI tools at least weekly save an average of 5.9 hours per week, the equivalent of 6 full weeks per school year.

In 2025, the global AI in education market was valued at $7.1 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach $136.8 billion by 2035.

As big as AI has become, we are still incredibly early when it comes to AI adoption.

Keep watching AI in education.


Sue: AI is making kids dumber.

It should be making them geniuses.

Introducing Koji, the first AI tutor that gets kids to actually think. 👇

items.title: BREAKING: President Trump's Situation Room meeting on a possible deal with Iran lasted for 2 hours, but did not end with a final decision on any new d...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: President Trump's Situation Room meeting on a possible deal with Iran lasted for 2 hours, but did not end with a final decision on any new deal with Iran, per NYT.

The Trump Administration believes it is "close" to an agreement but there are uncertainties including the unfreezing of Iranian funds.

items.title: Oil fell to $94 last night. Back at $98 today ! Long period of calm broken Iran's IRGC says it has retaliated to the US strike on an Iranian military ...
items.description_html:
Oil fell to $94 last night. Back at $98 today !

Long period of calm broken

Iran's IRGC says it has retaliated to the US strike on an Iranian military position in the Strait of Hormuz by attacking a US airbase in Kuwait. Yesterday, American forces shot down Iranian drones & hit a drone-control station near Bandar Abbas..

Wrt talks.. it's assertions & denials. Rinse & repeat👇

1. Iran's state broadcaster reported that it had seen a draft framework being negotiated with the US & that it includes restoring commercial shipping to prewar levels within a month.

2. The White House denied reports of a draft interim peace agreement & Trump said he was “not satisfied” with negotiations.

3. Trump asserted that no one nation would control the Strait of Hormuz - “Nobody’s going to control it. It’s international waters”; “The strait’s going to be open to everybody” and the US will “watch over it”

4. Iran rejected Trump's new comments saying the Strait remains permanently closed, with Iran retaining "full sovereign control" over it and "preserve its nuclear materials as a guarantee of the other party's compliance" in any future agreement..per a source close to Iran's Speaker of the Parliament.

#Nifty #BankNifty #IranWar‌ #OilPrices #India

@CNBCTV18News

items.title: I hosted @kanodiaankit12 on the podcast again. Last time we spoke, most micro caps were bleeding. This time, we went somewhere I didn't expect - a ful...
items.description_html:
I hosted @kanodiaankit12 on the podcast again.

Last time we spoke, most micro caps were bleeding. This time, we went somewhere I didn't expect - a full breakdown of consumption as a sector.

Food. Retail. Cinema. Amusement parks. Luggage. Hotels.
45 minutes of pure insights.

But the thing that hit me hardest had nothing to do with stock picks.

He said: "You're not an allocator of money. You're first an allocator of time."
Every investable rupee he has goes into equity. No FDs. No debt funds. Because he's clear he's still in asset creation mode.

His portfolio? 15 stocks max. His co-founder runs 5.

Concentrated. Intentional. Not 50 stocks dressed up as "diversification."
Most people in their 20s and 30s obsess over asset allocation. That's a preservation mindset. You can't preserve what you haven't built yet.

He also admitted the market has humbled him on stocks he's held for 3 years. No spin.

Even said he's considering selling one position just to see how he feels about the business without owning it.

That kind of self-awareness is rare.

Watch our full conversation here - https://youtu.be/TasQSPIhg08?si=9Kf6cDMVaGB3Kyb2

items.title: RT Madhav Chanchani: Policybazaar founders Yashish Dahiya and Alok Bansal have now collectively sold shares worth over Rs 3,100 crore since the compan...
items.description_html:
RT Madhav Chanchani
Policybazaar founders Yashish Dahiya and Alok Bansal have now collectively sold shares worth over Rs 3,100 crore since the company’s IPO in 2021.

This marks one of the largest instances of share monetisation by founders of India’s new-age listed companies.

The latest sale comes after Dahiya and Bansal sold a 0.8% stake in the company through a block deal worth Rs 665.38 crore on Friday.

They will continue to collectively own a little over 4% stake in the company.

The buyers were largely foreign institutional investors, including St. James's Place Emerging Markets Trust, Viridian Asia, Société Générale and Goldman Sachs.

Pronto in India does this with paid help. Shift in NYC does it free.

Same fundamental problem (embodied/physical AI needs real-world data), solved with inverted unit economics. In India the customer pays for the service, and the data is the byproduct. In NYC, the data pays for the service, and the customer gets it free.


shift: Today, we're launching shift. We're starting by cleaning your apartment in New York City, for free.

items.title: RT ValueEquity: I want to Compete with the opportunity set , I am an individual investor and as even Stan druckenmiller says , I actually want to comp...
items.description_html:
RT ValueEquity
I want to Compete with the opportunity set , I am an individual investor and as even Stan druckenmiller says , I actually want to compete with the best performing stocks for the three year period across segments , and that would mean the process based reward for me

Finding the 30-40% best performing themes of a 3 year cycle is what I try to accomplish , as an Individual investor it is less about alpha but scaling multifold Compete with opportunity set of best companies for incremental returns , your returns paradigm will change and that's when you will stop playing the small game

For allocators 2% alpha would mean a lot on multi thousand crore AUM , and yes it would mean a lot for us also , but not as much as scaling multifold by actually trying to capture the big winners

My outcomes are compared to with the depth of my coverage universe , was I able to participate in the 3-4 stocks out of the 10 best performing in the 2 year cycle runway , that gives me the edge to scale at an Individual investor level!

But to do this , and something I am constantly working on is the ability to have a broader and deeper coverage for themes with just the right agility to execute when there is opportunity , execution backed by coverage depth with margin of safety is what I keep trying to focus on !

I too am learning to sharpen the edge with time !

items.title: Interesting slide from KSG International - Margins Can Mislead: Track EBITDA per Ton In metal-linked companies, falling margins can sometimes be misle...
items.description_html:
Interesting slide from KSG International - Margins Can Mislead: Track EBITDA per Ton

In metal-linked companies, falling margins can sometimes be misleading.

Why?

Because higher raw material prices inflate revenue.

The company may still earn the same value-add per ton.

So EBITDA margin % falls, but absolute EBITDA per ton remains stable.

Always check unit economics, not just percentage margins.

items.title: Key insights from this note on Titan & gold duty changes The key learning is that custom duty hikes alone are not always negative for organized jewell...
items.description_html:
Key insights from this note on Titan & gold duty changes

The key learning is that custom duty hikes alone are not always negative for organized jewellers

What matters more is whether the duty hike is combined with harsh regulatory controls

In 2013-14, the sector faced a very tough regulatory phase for nearly 12-18 months

Government was trying to control gold imports because gold buying was hurting CAD and the currency. During this period, multiple restrictions were introduced:
- Custom duty increased from 2% to 10%
- 80:20 rule was implemented
- Temporary ban on gold coins & bars
- Restrictions on gold on lease

The biggest issue for organized jewellers was the restriction on gold on lease

This increased working capital needs, interest cost & hedging expenses

That is why 2013-14 was a difficult period for the sector

But the 2022 duty hike was very different.

Post Covid, custom duty was increased from 10% to 15%, but organized jewellery companies benefited because the broader environment was favourable

Demand was strong, formalization was improving & organized players were gaining share

During 2017-18, when gold prices were largely flat, Titan delivered strong growth, with around 25% REV CAGR between FY17-FY19 & more than 35% EBIT CAGR.

Similarly, during 2021-22, when gold prices were flat, Titan delivered around 35% REV CAGR & around 60% EBIT CAGR during FY21-FY23.

This shows that stable gold prices are actually positive for jewellers.

When gold prices become stable after a sharp rise, consumers adjust to the new price level & buying momentum comes back.

The recent problem was that FY25 & FY26 saw sharp gold inflation, which impacted footfall and buyer growth. But once prices stabilize, demand can normalize again.

src : MOFSL

items.title: Interesting perspective from Jefferies on the current AI cycle They are saying AI wil be transformative in the long run, but the current capex cycle m...
items.description_html:
Interesting perspective from Jefferies on the current AI cycle

They are saying AI wil be transformative in the long run, but the current capex cycle may still destroy capital in the short run

That is exactly what happened during earlier technology booms

The internet changed the world, but many dot com companies still destroyed capital

Railroads changed America, but many railroad investors also lost money during the boom bust cycle

The same risk exists in AI.

The key concern is that US hyperscalers are spending aggressively because of fear of disruption. Nobody wants to be left behind, so everyone keeps increasing AI capex.

But the question is whether the future cash flows will justify this level of investment.

Another interesting point is that China’s AI capex is much lower compared to the US. Jefferies highlights that Chinese AI capex is only around 18% of the projected US hyperscaler capex.

This raises a key question:
Is the US overbuilding AI infrastructure?

So far, the biggest winners in this cycle have been the picks & shovels - chips, memory, servers, networking, power, cooling and data centre ecosystem.

items.title: Why comparing only P/S does not give the right picture on valuations A company with 1% net profit margin and a company with 25% net profit margin cann...
items.description_html:
Why comparing only P/S does not give the right picture on valuations

A company with 1% net profit margin and a company with 25% net profit margin cannot be compared only on P/S.

At first glance, a company trading at 1x P/S may look very cheap.

But if its net profit margin is only 1%, then that 1x P/S actually translates into 100x P/E.

So the real valuation depends on margins.

Relationship between P/S, margin and P/E

Formula is simple:
P/E = P/S divided by Net Profit Margin

So if a company trades at 5x P/S and earns 10% net margin:
5 / 10% = 50x P/E

Similarly, if a company trades at 10x P/S and earns 50% net margin, then the P/E is 20x.

This is why P/S is useful only when businesses are at an early stage or when profits are temporarily depressed.

For profitable companies, P/S should never be used in isolation.

3. Alphabet (Google): 11x

items.title: Bharti Airtel is a cashflow machine right now ! India operating FCF, defined as EBITDAaL minus capex, stood at 41,500 Cr in FY26🔥 Con. business is ...
items.description_html:
Bharti Airtel is a cashflow machine right now !

India operating FCF, defined as EBITDAaL minus capex, stood at 41,500 Cr in FY26🔥

Con. business is generating more than $5 Bn of FCF

Management has clearly laid out capital allocation priorities.

Priority 1 - Invest in the core business

Priority 2 - Deleveraging
Net debt to EBITDAaL has reduced to 1.1x
With rising FCF, Airtel has room to reduce leverage further.

Priority 3 - Growth adjacencies
Airtel is investing in three major adjacencies:
- Data centers
- Financial services
- Airtel Cloud

Priority 4 - Increasing Shareholder payouts via dividends & buybacks
Dividend increased from 16/share to 24/share

Nxtra is targeting 1 GW data center capacity over the next few years

Management indicated capex should remain broadly around current levels

Bharti Airtel is moving from a telecom operator to a diversified digital infrastructure

Next phase of value creation will come from ARPU expansion, homes, B2B digital, data centers, cloud, financial services & Africa.

items.title: Bharti Airtel Management on ARPU Gopal Vittal’s clearly said that the price architecture in India is broken Management compared this with global mark...
items.description_html:
Bharti Airtel Management on ARPU

Gopal Vittal’s clearly said that the price architecture in India is broken

Management compared this with global markets:
India unlimited data - Rs 340-350
US unlimited data - $60-70
Europe unlimited data - €35

At Rs 340-350, customers are capped out with unlimited data plans

This means even if data consumption keeps rising, revenue per GB does not increase meaningfully

The key point is that India remains structurally under-monetised on data pricing

Management also made an important point that the rich are paying less than they should, while the poor are probably paying as much as they need to

ARPU stood at 257 in Q4, Management was clearly not satisfied with the Q4 ARPU movement

Larger point is that Airtel wants to accelerate ARPU growth from here

items.title: RT Luis Garicano 🇪🇺🇺🇦: Back from a German conference. Stayed at a not-cheap hotel (fittingly called "Bad Hotel"): hand towels instead of b...
items.description_html:
RT Luis Garicano 🇪🇺🇺🇦
Back from a German conference. Stayed at a not-cheap hotel (fittingly called "Bad Hotel"): hand towels instead of body towels, no air-conditioning in 30+ degree weather, a printed request that I skip room cleaning. I checked last night the instant electricity mix: most electricity produced was from coal.

Germany has made the choice to be poor. It could have cheap, safe, abundant energy from nuclear. Instead it has chosen expensive, high carbon energy.

items.title: RT abey varghese: Meanwhile, fresh ideas — especially around artificial intelligence — are nowhere near what firms in China, South Korea and Taiwan ...
items.description_html:
RT abey varghese
Meanwhile, fresh ideas — especially around artificial intelligence — are nowhere near what firms in China, South Korea and Taiwan are offering investors. The mercantilist capitalist class that straddles India’s economy because of its proximity to political power can’t think beyond data centers, which will merely channel cheap solar energy into running AI models. The tokens they mint at home will power Western coding agents like Claude and Codex, eroding the pricing power of domestic outsourcing companies. While the Korean semiconductor industry is in the middle of an unprecedented wage boom, six million programmers in India are facing a funk, writes @andymukherjee70 https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-05-27/an-iran-deal-won-t-plug-india-s-capital-or-ai-gaps?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter

items.description_html:
RT Fermat's Library
Fun fact: Romania’s president, Nicușor Dan, was one of the 11 students to solve the legendary Problem 6 at the 1988 IMO.

He scored a perfect 42/42 that year, alongside future Fields Medalist Ngô Bảo Châu. Ravi Vakil also solved it.

Terence Tao, aged just 13, got 1/7 on that problem, aced almost everything else, and still won gold.

items.title: RT Kayezad E Adajania: DO SIPs WORK? Of the 295 actively managed, diversified equity schemes with a two-year track record, 26% haven’t made any money...
items.description_html:
RT Kayezad E Adajania
DO SIPs WORK?

Of the 295 actively managed, diversified equity schemes with a two-year track record, 26% haven’t made any money in the last two years if you had started your SIP then, according to ACE MF data. Nearly 54% have failed to beat 5% returns. Just 1% of schemes have made returns in excess of 10% if you had started your SIP two years back.

These are precisely the times when investors question the veracity and wisdom of SIPs. Which is where our annual @ET_Wealth - @CrisilLimited annual SIP Study 2026 comes in.

If you continue your SIPs for 10 years, you won’t lose money. But the probability of incurring losses starts to decline after 4 years.

+ This year we have also analysed how long it takes for SIPs to recover after market crash. This is for those who panic and stop their SIPs in turbulence.

But the bigger story is how investors actually behave during a crisis. Our cover story features three individuals who didn’t stop their SIPs despite a personal crisis, and two who did and paid the price.

My story in this week’s @ET_Wealth. Let me know in the comments how you’ve reacted during a crisis; I would love to hear your stories.

items.title: RT Tijori: We ran as screen trying to figure out casting suppliers exposed to Tata and candidates for new suppliers Here are the top listed candidates
items.description_html:
RT Tijori
We ran as screen trying to figure out casting suppliers exposed to Tata and candidates for new suppliers

Here are the top listed candidates


Markets by Zerodha: Tata's problem is different. It is not short on factory space. But the Tata Sierra, which has seen strong bookings, is stuck because its casting suppliers for the new engine cannot ramp fast enough. Tata is bringing in additional suppliers to plug the gap.

items.title: RT Aaron Levie: Again, maybe counterintuitive, but in the majority of conversations I have with CIOs, CTOs, and CEOs in large enterprises, they are ei...
items.description_html:
RT Aaron Levie
Again, maybe counterintuitive, but in the majority of conversations I have with CIOs, CTOs, and CEOs in large enterprises, they are either growing due to AI (in new job functions like FDEs, engineering, etc.) or at a minimum reinvesting efficiency savings back into the business in new areas (sales, marketing, etc.).

David Solomon, CEO of Goldman Sachs, articulated this perfectly in a NYTimes OpEd last week. The AI boom is both creating all new jobs in the build out of AI systems and the implementation across sectors, but also freeing up dollars to invest in areas that have been underfunded or have more demand now because of AI.

Most businesses have been constrained by how much software they can produce at a given cost, how many sales reps they can hire, how many marketing campaigns they can run, how they can do outbound customer success motions with enough tailoring, how they can find more risk in their business and prevent it, and 100s of other things.

When AI makes it possible to do more of this, investment goes back into the business. The companies that better serve their customers win over the long run, and those that just try and find savings end up doing worse.


David Sacks: Apollo’s Chief Economist: Zero Evidence of AI-Related Job Losses

This marks one of the largest instances of share monetisation by founders of India’s new-age listed companies.

They will continue to collectively own a little over 4% stake in the company.

items.title: Swiggy is in damage-control mode after shareholders rejected its proposal to give more board seats to the founders and management team last week. The ...
items.description_html:
Swiggy is in damage-control mode after shareholders rejected its proposal to give more board seats to the founders and management team last week.

The company said in an exchange filing last night that the proposed changes were not about “concentration of power”, but part of an effort to make Swiggy an Indian-owned and controlled entity (IOCC).

Swiggy added that board rights are not in perpetuity and were approved by its independent directors.

Co-founder and Group CEO Sriharsha Majety admitted in an interview with The Economic Times that the company could have “handled this better through engagement” and “communicated more effectively”.

Majety has also said he wants to put the proposal to a vote again and believes it will eventually pass.

Let’s understand Swiggy’s board structure right now.

- Sriharsha Majety is currently the only management representative on the board after co-founder Nandan Reddy stepped down earlier this year.

- Amsterdam-listed Prosus, which owns over 21% in Swiggy, now has two representatives on the board - India head Ashutosh Sharma and Renan Pinto (who joined last week).

- The board also has four independent directors, including chairman Anand Kripalu.

However, independent directors, even if they are Indian residents, are not counted toward Indian control norms.

This meant Swiggy needed at least three management representatives on the board to outweigh the two directors representing foreign investor Prosus.

Proxy advisory firms argued that board representation should be linked to shareholding. Majety and co-founder Phani Kishan Addepalli together own less than 5% of Swiggy.

Failure to become an Indian-controlled entity could delay Swiggy Instamart’s shift from a marketplace structure to an owned-inventory model similar to Blinkit.

The owned-inventory model has given market leader Blinkit several advantages.

By owning inventory directly, Blinkit captures the full retail spread, improving gross margins already by 3% and is expected to improve net margin by 1%.

Owning the goods also allows Blinkit to book the full value of sold items as revenue instead of earning only a marketplace commission.

It will be interesting to see how this impacts Swiggy’s profitability and break-even ambitions from now on.

items.description_html:
RT Chad P. Bown
.@Kramerbooks is a Washington DC institution. Located just off Dupont Circle, the bookstore is just around the corner from my @PIIE office of the last 10 years. I stop in there frequently. But this is the first time my book was on its shelves.

This is Llalan. She walked me over to our new book and patiently let me soak it all in.

HOW TO WIN A TRADE WAR is here.

items.title: To the extent AI adoption improves firm performance, faster adopters are likely to gain competitive advantages over slower rivals. Faster AI adoption ...
items.description_html:
To the extent AI adoption improves firm performance, faster adopters are likely to gain competitive advantages over slower rivals. Faster AI adoption could increase concentration of US employment in large firms by lowering costs relative to small firms.
https://www.piie.com/blogs/realtime-economics/2026/adoption-ai-industrial-sectors
